content:
This is a real experience from building my Final Year Project at UMT. Written to help other developers avoid the same confusion.
"Building Firebase Authentication + Firestore Integration for a Mental Health AI Chatbot"
Introduction
I'm Ayesha, a final year CS student at UMT. For my FYP, I built "Neurodivergent Assistant" an AI chatbot designed to provide mental health support for people with autism and ADHD who struggle with verbal communication.
And honestly? We almost broke it with Firebase.
Not in a catastrophic way. But in that frustrating way where everything looks fine on the surface, but nothing's actually working underneath.
The Problem That Made Us Lose Our Minds
Here's what happened:
A user would sign up with their email and password. Firebase Authentication would create an account perfectly. Our login page worked. Everything seemed fine.
But then our Admin Dashboard showed zero users. Empty. Nothing.
We spent LITERAL HOURS on this. Like, sitting in the lab at 2 AM, refreshing the Firebase console over and over, thinking something was catastrophically wrong. Was the backend down? Did someone accidentally delete the data? Is Firebase even connected?
We tried everything. And I mean everything.
Finally, we traced the entire signup flow from React frontend → Flask backend → Firebase. That's when it hit us like a brick:
Firebase Auth and Firestore are two completely different databases.
When a user signed up, we were only saving them to Firebase Authentication (the login system). We weren't creating a user document in Firestore (where we store actual user data like name, mood history, preferences).
Two databases. Two separate saves. We were only doing one.
How did we not catch this immediately? I honestly don't know. But we didn't.
How We Fixed It (And It Was Stupidly Simple)
Once we got it, the solution was actually simple. But man, it took a while to get there.
After Firebase Auth created the account, we added one more step: capture the user's unique ID (uid) and immediately create a corresponding document in the Firestore users collection.
What it looks like:
```
1. User enters email & password
2. Firebase Auth creates account → returns uid
3. Take that uid
4. Create new document in Firestore/users/{uid}
5. Save user profile data there (name, email, preferences)
```
We refreshed the Firebase console. Users were there. All of them.
I remember just staring at the screen for a second like "that's it?"
Yeah. That was it.
What This Actually Taught Me
Authentication ≠ User Data
This was the mindshift that changed everything. Firebase Authentication is just the login system. It verifies credentials. But it doesn't store your user profile, mood history, or any of that stuff.
Firestore is your actual database. It's where everything lives.
If you only use Firebase Auth and forget Firestore, your users can log in but the system has no idea who they are beyond their email. They're ghosts in the machine.
Always Capture and Save the UID
The UID is the connection between the two. When a user is created in Firebase Auth, grab that UID immediately and use it as the key in Firestore. Everything stems from that one ID.
We learned this the hard way. But now whenever I set up a new user, I don't even think about it. UID → capture → save → done.
Real Time Sync Matters
We could have let signup happen asynchronously (Firebase Auth first, then save to Firestore later). But what if the server crashes between the two? The user exists in Auth but not in your database.
So we made sure both happen in the same transaction. User created in Auth → immediately saved in Firestore. No gaps. No ghost accounts.
If You’re Building With Firebase, Here’s What I’d Tell You
1. Think about where data lives. Auth for login. Firestore for everything else. Seriously, keep them separate in your head.
2. Test the full flow. Don't just check if login works. Check if the user actually appears in your dashboard. Check if their data is really there.
3. Use the Firebase console.*Go actually look at your Firestore documents. Don't just assume they're there. See them with your own eyes.
4. Document the UID. Make it the primary key in Firestore. Everything connects through it. This is non-negotiable.
What's Next
We've since built mood tracking, drawing features, stress ball interactions .. all storing data in Firestore through that same UID foundation.
The signup flow that confused us for hours became the solid base for everything else. Sometimes the annoying bugs are the ones that teach you the most.
And now when we onboard new users, it just works.
Because we got it right the first time. Eventually.
That’s it. Hope this saves you some debugging time.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

"The Admin Dashboard after the fix — now showing all 74 registered users, therapists, and caregivers."
